questions.question: What is the smallest possible surface for the ModelAdapter interface?
options: generate(prompt, task) returning text plus optional confidence and per-token nll
complete, embed, classify, summarise
init, login, generate, close
load_weights, generate, save_weights
questions.explanation: One method covers any adapter. Everything else is optional decoration. The runner does not need a richer surface.

questions.question: Why does the runner accept a parallel flag rather than always running in parallel?
options: Parallel execution is unsafe in CPython
Tests need deterministic execution order; the flag lets them switch to sequential
Parallel execution requires GPU
ThreadPoolExecutor is not stdlib
questions.explanation: Tests want reproducibility. Production wants throughput. The flag splits those concerns without two code paths.

questions.question: How does the runner determine the correct flag for the calibration buffer?
options: It calls the adapter twice and compares outputs
It uses exact_match-style metrics at threshold 1.0 and graded metrics at threshold 0.5 by default
It uses the average bin confidence
It uses Brier decomposition
questions.explanation: The threshold is metric-aware: exact_match-style metrics use a near-1.0 cutoff, while graded metrics use 0.5 by default.

questions.question: Why does the runner build EvalRun records and hand them to the aggregator instead of computing the leaderboard inline?
options: EvalRun is more memory-efficient
It keeps the runner agnostic to the aggregator and lets the leaderboard layer evolve without touching the runner
The aggregator is faster on numpy arrays than dataclasses
EvalRun is required by JSON Schema
questions.explanation: Composition over inlining. The runner produces records; the aggregator owns the leaderboard math. Each lesson owns one concern.

questions.question: What is the role of the perplexity block in the final JSON envelope?
options: It is required for the leaderboard ranking
It is the input to the calibration report
It carries the held-out language modelling number per model, independent of task scoring
It is computed from the bootstrap CI
questions.explanation: Perplexity sits next to the leaderboard, not inside it. It uses the adapter's per-call token NLL and token counts.

questions.question: What is the clean-run exit criterion of the self-terminating demo?
options: Every task validated, every task scored, calibration aggregated, and rule-based adapter strictly above the random adapter on the leaderboard
Every task scored above 0.9
ECE below 0.05 for every model
Bootstrap CI excludes zero for every pair
questions.explanation: The demo enforces the contract end to end: every layer ran and the most accurate adapter is ranked accordingly.
